column5: I think more code will be written with the help of AI, so in the near future more code review will be required, because with AI assistance more code will be written, which can be all over the place with the current AI models. Further in the future is questionable (in my opinion, a lot is depended if writing source code with AI is worth the energy) if the AI will be able to write clean and working code consistently, maybe less code review will be needed.
column6: We will have to decide if we want to write code with AI or review code with AI (code review is more energy efficient than generating the code in my opinion) I don't think both at the same time will work, because there can be instances, where the programmer generates bad code with the AI, and a mistake is kept in, and because of time shortage the reviewer also uses AI to review the same code, and the AI doesn't catch the mistake because it generated it.

column5: More code to review in general due to the agentic generation. Some modern language are not well suited for code review in browser, because many things are implicit and visible only with IDE assistance (e.g. Kotlin, inlays for inferred types and `this` type). Complex build systems where a single source code unit can be re-used in different contexts e.g. in modules with different dependencies.

AI will produce a tons of code with various quality. Reading that code will be more important than actually writing it. We need better tools to quickly read, review and adjust. The primary target of review will be AI Agent.

column2: About the same on code review
column5:
I think that in the next five years, the amount of time we spend on code review will not change much. We might be able to produce more code due to agents and LLMs, but at the same time we will probably be able to review more with the help of agents and LLMs. There might be an increase in the amount of code that goes through the code review, but that increase in that amount will not necessarily result in an increased code review time.

I also do not think that code review will disappear completely due to agents or LLMs. There will be people who will need to review the code which creates the agents, but also people who review agent written code when it is important. 

It is worth noting, that when we speak of “code review”, my understanding of it is that a person reviews another person's code, with or without the help of additional tools. When I am asking an agent or an LLM to write me a piece of code required to complete my task, I would not consider it code review. I think for a while humans will still monitor the output of the individual agents and then submit the code, after they make their changes if necessary, to the code review.
column6: Code review will continue to be an important part of sharing the information with other team members and discussing possible improvements. We might be to review more code written by agents on our behalf, but the code review between two people will take the same time because there will be tools which can process more code and help with the review.
column10: Code review will continue to be an important part of sharing the information with other team members and discussing possible improvements. We might be to review more code written by agents on our behalf, but the code review between two people will take the same time because there will be tools which can process more code and help with the review.
